title: Why Am I Always Tired on a Diet? (Energy Crash Causes and Solutions)
introduction: Diet fatigue is a major reason people quit. If you are always tired on a diet, your calorie deficit, macro balance, sleep, or recovery strategy may need adjustment.

sections.heading: 😴 Why Diets Cause Low Energy
sections.content:
| Cause | Fatigue Mechanism |
| --- | --- |
| Deficit too large | Low available energy |
| Too little carbohydrate for training | Reduced performance and recovery |
| Low sleep quality | Hormonal and cognitive fatigue |
| Micronutrient gaps | Lower perceived energy |

Tiredness is often a signal to adjust plan quality, not quit.

sections.heading: How to Fix Diet Fatigue
sections.content:
Use this order:

1) Moderate your deficit with [calorie deficit calculator](/tools/calorie-deficit-calculator)
2) Keep protein consistent with [protein calculator](/tools/protein-calculator)
3) Time carbs around training
4) Improve sleep and stress hygiene

Related: [why do i feel hungry all the time](/blog/why-do-i-feel-hungry-all-the-time).

sections.heading: When to Consider a Diet Break
sections.content:
Signs you may need 1-2 weeks at maintenance:

• Persistent low energy
• Training performance decline
• Irritability and poor sleep

Text CTA: Track energy, sleep, and intake in Eati daily so you can see whether fatigue is from nutrition, recovery, or both.

midArticleCta: Use Eati to track energy and nutrition together so your fat-loss plan stays effective without burnout.
conclusion: Being tired on a diet is common but not something you should ignore. A moderate deficit, adequate protein, better recovery, and smart tracking can restore energy and preserve fat-loss progress.
metaTitle: Why Am I Always Tired on a Diet? Energy Fix Guide
metaDescription: Find out why diet fatigue happens and how to fix low energy during weight loss with better deficit size, macros, sleep, and recovery.
targetKeyword: why am i always tired on a diet

faqs.question: Should I increase calories if I am exhausted on a diet?
faqs.answer: Often yes, especially if the deficit is aggressive. A smaller deficit can improve adherence, training quality, and long-term fat-loss results.
